data.title: Sibanye-Stillwater upgraded at HSBC, seeing extended PGM market deficit
data.description:
Sibanye-Stillwater (SBSW) +7.4% in Friday's trading to its highest in nearly four years as HSBC upgraded the miner to Buy from Hold with a $24.80/share price target for its ADRs, raised from $13.30, believing the company will benefit from rising prices for platinum group metals and gold.

PGM prices have increased sharply, and HSBC analyst Shilan Modi believes the rally reflects underlying bullish fundamentals, noting mine supply has been limited as previous low prices reduced investment and encouraged restructuring, thus sustained high prices may be needed ahead of an increase in mine supply.

The analyst said he expects the market deficit in the 3E PGMs - platinum, palladium, and rhodium - to extend into 2026, including a widening deficit for platinum (XPTUSD:CUR) over the next five years.

PGM miners have indicated they required higher prices to sustain or raise production volumes, and Modi said prices have moved above the required levels; the analyst said PGM miners typically prioritize deleveraging and increasing cash returns at this phase of the cycle, but he sees a possible increase in reinvestment capital in the next 12-24 months as the companies seek to stabilize volumes and potentially seek growth over the next decade.

data.title: Glacier Bancorp targets mid-50s efficiency ratio in 2026 amid acquisition-driven growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Glacier Bancorp, Inc. (GBCI) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Randall Chesler, President and CEO, stated that 2025 was "a transformative year for Glacier Bancorp," highlighting the successful closures of the Bank of Idaho and Guaranty Bank & Trust acquisitions, which expanded the company's footprint into Idaho and Texas. Chesler emphasized, "These markets offer strong growth potential and fit seamlessly with our long-term growth strategy." The company ended the year with $32 billion in total assets, a record for Glacier Bancorp.
  • Chesler reported, "Net income was $63.8 million for the quarter, including the $36 million of expenses related to our 2025 acquisitions," and for the full year, "Net income for 2025 was $239 million, an increase of $48.9 million or 26% from the prior year."
  • Ron Copher, Executive VP, CFO & Secretary: "Q2 through Q4, I would estimate it will range, and this is for each of the 3 remaining quarters, $187 million to $192 million...the full year guide would be $750 million to, say, $766 million for the full year. Again, that's core operating expense."
  • Byron Pollan, Senior VP & Treasurer, added, "We've seen tremendous progress in our net interest margin. We've got great momentum, and we continue to see momentum ahead of us."

Outlook

  • Management is guiding for core noninterest expense in Q1 2026 of $189 million to $193 million, with expectations that expense will "step down" over Q2, Q3, and Q4.
  • The full-year 2026 core operating expense is projected in the range of $750 million to $766 million.
  • Efficiency ratio is expected to reach "mid-50s, 54% to 55%, which is our traditional range" by the second half of 2026, according to Pollan.
  • On loan growth, Tom Dolan, Senior VP & Chief Credit Officer, stated, "We're looking at low to mid-single digits for the full year," with a record pipeline at the start of 2026 and potential to be "towards the higher end of that range."

Financial Results

  • Total assets ended 2025 at $32 billion, with a loan portfolio of $21 billion at year-end, up $2 billion from the prior quarter and $3.7 billion for the year.
  • Total deposits reached $24.6 billion, increasing $2.7 billion from the prior quarter and $4 billion over the year.
  • Net interest margin for the quarter was 3.58%, an increase of 19 basis points from the prior quarter.
  • Diluted earnings per share for Q4 2025 was $0.49; for the full year, $1.99 per share, up $0.31 (18%) from the prior year.
  • Noninterest income for the quarter totaled $40 million, up $5 million over the prior quarter.
  • Noninterest expense for the quarter was $195 million, up $26.8 million primarily due to acquisition costs.
  • Tangible book value per share increased to $21, up 12% year-over-year.

Q&A

  • David Feaster, Raymond James: Questioned the pace of organic loan growth and Guaranty’s contribution. Tom Dolan responded that seasonal slowdowns were expected but noted, "We are now at a record level of our pipeline early this year...a growing piece of the production is related to construction."
  • Feaster asked about margin trajectory and dependency on Fed action. Byron Pollan stated, "We have a lot of programmatic structural repricing drivers...we're not in any way Fed dependent."
  • Questions from Andrew Terrell, Stephens, and Kelly Motta, KBW, focused on expense guidance and loan yields. Copher confirmed, "Q2 through Q4, I would estimate...$187 million to $192 million," and Chesler noted higher new loan pricing margins.
  • Matthew Clark, Piper Sandler, asked about deposit costs and cost savings from Guaranty. Pollan said, "the uptick that you saw was from the acquisition, and we do expect to see a declining deposit costs from here."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised pointed questions on loan growth, margin sustainability, cost guidance, and integration progress, reflecting a neutral to slightly positive tone with focus on clarity and confirmation of growth drivers.
  • Management’s tone remained confident throughout, with frequent reminders of progress and discipline. Chesler’s closing statement, "Very excited about the trends here and the growth into '26," and Pollan’s assertion, "We've seen tremendous progress in our net interest margin," reinforced this confidence.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management maintained a steady, constructive tone with increased focus on integration outcomes and expense discipline.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw the completion of two strategic acquisitions, while the previous quarter only noted the completion of Bank of Idaho and the closing of Guaranty Bank & Trust.
  • Guidance for expense run rates became more specific, with management projecting $750 million to $766 million in core operating expenses for 2026, compared to more preliminary expense discussions last quarter.
  • Margin expansion continued, with the net interest margin increasing to 3.58% from 3.39% in Q3, and management reaffirmed the 4% target for the second half of 2026.
  • Analysts’ focus shifted more toward the integration of recent acquisitions, near-term efficiency gains, and expense control, while management expressed greater confidence in achieving traditional efficiency levels.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged seasonality in loan growth, early term payoffs in 2025, and integration costs associated with acquisitions.
  • Copher noted, "Q1 is typically our higher first quarter because we have the merit pay increases, employment taxes and then it will drop down."
  • Analysts queried about the sustainability of loan yield expansion and the realization of cost saves, with management indicating careful monitoring and discipline in both areas.

Final Takeaway

Glacier Bancorp closed 2025 with record asset growth, two significant acquisitions, and expanding geographic reach. The company detailed clear guidance for expense control, targeting a return to mid-50s efficiency ratios in 2026, and expects continued loan and margin growth supported by robust pipelines and repricing momentum. Management remains confident in achieving integration milestones and delivering further shareholder value in the coming year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Apple plans to complete rollout of ad expansion in App Store search by late March
data.description:
Last year, Apple (AAPL) revealed it was going to allow for increased advertising by developers in its App Store during user searches in 2026.

The Cupertino-based company has now confirmed the advertising opportunity expansion will be available in all markets by the end of March.

"When a user searches on the App Store, your ad can appear at the top of their search results," Apple said. "And starting in March 2026, we’ll be introducing more ads to increase opportunity in search results."

About 65% of app downloads in the App Store occur directly after a search.

"To help give advertisers more opportunities to drive downloads from search results, Apple Ads will introduce additional ads across search queries," the company said.

Advertisers do not need to modify their campaign to be eligible for new positions. The ad format will be the same in any position, using a default product page or custom product page and an optional deep link. Advertisers will be billed as usual based on their pricing model, which is either cost per tap or cost per install. Advertisers can rely on default ads or create their own custom variation.

These ads are then matched to user searches based on relevant keywords. 

"If your app isn't relevant to what the user is searching for, it won't be displayed—regardless of how much you may be willing to pay," Apple said. "Apple Ads considers both relevance and bids and doesn't put apps into auctions if they're not a good match."

The App Store ecosystem generates hundreds of billions in revenue in developer billings and sales.

"Over the last five years, the size of the App Store ecosystem has nearly tripled from $142 billion in 2019 to $406 billion last year, and earnings for U.S.-based developers also more than doubled," Apple said, citing a study by the Boston University Questrom School of Business. "Small developers in particular have done exceptionally well as their earnings increased by 76 percent between 2021 and 2024."

data.title: Tractor Supply traffic soars ahead of massive winter storm
data.description:
Tractor Supply (TSCO) is seeing a surge of store and online traffic ahead of Winter Storm Fern, according to channel checks from Jefferies. The potentially historic winter storm is expected to blanket a large part of the U.S., including states in the South and Southwest not accustomed to snow and ice.

Analyst Jonathan Matuszewski pointed to a spike in daily web traffic to Tractor Supply's (TSCO) website from consumers in Texas, North Carolina, and Florida. He noted that Tractor Supply (TSCO) locations in East Texas have shelves cleared, while Oklahoma stores are out of propane tanks. "A store manager for the Tractor Supply in Lexington, North Carolina, indicated wood & fuel-burning stoves, kerosene, and generators are flying off the shelves for homes, and heat lamps for animals are seeing similar demand," updated Matuszewski. 

Matuszewski and his team believe that demand tied to the storm could boost Q1 top-line results.

Shares of Tractor Supply (TSCO) were up 1.8% in Friday afternoon trading. The retail stock is up about 8.7% on a year-to-date basis.

data.title: Sanara MedTech expects Q4 revenue between $27.2M and $27.7M
data.description:
• Sanara MedTech (SMTI) on Friday said net revenue for the fourth quarter of 2025 is expected to be in the range of $27.2 million to $27.7 million, an increase of approximately 3% to 5%, compared to $26.3 million in the fourth quarter of 2024.
  • The company experienced growth in sales of BIASURGE Advanced Surgical Solution in the fourth quarter of 2024, as a result of supply chain issues and shortages of intravenous fluids and saline solutions experienced by the broader industry due to Hurricane Helene. Excluding approximately $1.8 million of BIASURGE sales in the fourth quarter of 2024, net revenue in the fourth quarter of 2025 is expected to increase approximately 11% to 13% year-over-year.
  • Net revenue for the full year 2025 is expected to be in the range of $102.7 million to $103.2 million, an increase of approximately 19%, compared to $86.7 million for the full year 2024.
  • Excluding the aforementioned $1.8 million of BIASURGE sales in the fourth quarter 2024, net revenue for the full year 2025 is expected to increase approximately 21% to 22% year-over-year.
  • Consensus for FY25 and Q4 revenues are $102.51M and $26.98M.
  • As of December 31, 2025, the company had cash of approximately $16.6 million and long-term debt of $46.0 million, compared to cash of $15.9 million and long-term debt of $30.7 million as of December 31, 2024.26.
  • Full Year 2026 Financial Guidance: The company expects full year 2026 net revenue to range from $116 million to $121 million, representing growth of approximately 13% to 17%, compared to the midpoint of the expected net revenue range for the full year 2025. 
  • SMTI is -4.42% to $21.26.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Netflix set to snap six straight sessions of losses
data.description:
Netflix (NFLX) shares were on track to snap six straight sessions of losses on Friday, as the stock rose nearly 3% to $86.04 in afternoon trading.

The streaming giant lost about 6% in the preceding six sessions. Overall, the stock rose over 5% last year, underperforming the nearly 17% rise in the broader S&P 500 Index.

NFLX is down nearly 9% over the past one month.

Netflix began its downward trend from Jan. 14, 2026. The stock closed 2% lower on Thursday at $83.54. Until now, January has not been a favorable month for the company, as the stock has just closed in green thrice, compared to the 11 sessions in red.

Netflix was in focus this week after posting fourth-quarter results that narrowly beat expectations. However, shares tumbled as the company’s bidding war with Paramount (PKSY) to take over Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) took the limelight.

Analysts, in general, found Netflix’s quarterly results somewhat “mixed” and “underwhelming.” However, they said that the streaming giant is poised for long-term growth and that the current weakness is 'a buying opportunity' for investors.

Looking at Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating, NFLX has a Hold rating with a score of 3.03 out of 5. The company received an A+ in the prospect of profitability, while it got a D- in the valuation factor.

Turning to the Wall Street community, 28 analysts gave NFLX a Buy and above, 14 analysts have given the stock a Hold recommendation, and two recommended Strong Sell. Seeking Alpha analysts are also cautious and see the stock as a Hold.

Earlier this week, Netflix made a revised all-cash offer for Warner Bros. Discovery at $27.75 per share. 

A recent Seeking Alpha analysis said that Netflix could be forced to match Paramount’s $30/share offer if it wants to win the bid.

“In my view, this could increase bridge financing and pressure the 2026 margin expectations. A downgrade from the 31.5% operating margin could hurt the stock,” it added.

data.title: Intel's tool spending to increase; Capex commitments at $9.1B for 2026
data.description:
Intel (INTC) said it will spend more on tools in 2026 than it did in 2025, a potentially positive sign for chip equipment making companies.

Intel's CFO David Zinsner said on the company's earnings call that previously they said capital expenditure, or CapEx, would be down, but are now planning for a range of flat to down slightly and for expenditures to be more weighted to the first half. 

"It's actually down significantly in space. So we're spending a lot less in space. We think we have a good footprint in terms of cleanroom. And what we're devoting more of our dollars to is tool. So we are ramping up tool spending quite a bit in '26 relative to '25 to address this supply shortfall as well," said Zinsner.

Zinsner noted that CapEx in 2026 would be to support demand in 2027 and beyond.

Intel's stock fell about 16% on Friday after the company's earnings results revealed a weaker-than-expected outlook for the quarter in progress and supply constraints continue to affect gross margins.

Intel said in its 10-K regulatory filing that as of Dec. 27, 2025, it had commitments for capital expenditures of $9.1B for 2026 and had $3.7B in capital expenditures committed in the long term.

The company noted that commitments for capital expenditures totaled $12.8B as of Dec. 27, 2025  versus $20B as of Dec. 28, 2024.

On a query that competitors, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) and Samsung Electronics (SSNLF) are aggressively slotting for equipment delivery, if Intel too would do so,  Zinsner noted that the company is doing so currently. 

"I mean we are aggressively getting tools on Intel 710, Intel 3 18A, that is happening. And we will be increasing our wafer starts as aggressively as possible on those nodes. What we're holding back on is 14A because 14A is really linked to foundry customers, and it does not make sense to build out significant capacity there until we know that we have the customers that will accept that demand,"  Zinsner noted.

Intel X710 is the company's series of 10 Gigabit Ethernet network adapters designed for servers and data centers. Meanwhile, Intel 3 is the company's 3nm-class semiconductor process, and Intel 18A is the company's 2nm-class manufacturing process.

There was no change in the company's disclosure about Intel 14A — the company's next-generation, sub-2nm manufacturing process technology.

The company said in the filing that "if we are unable to secure a significant external customer and meet important customer milestones for Intel 14A, we face the prospect that it will not be economical to develop and manufacture Intel 14A and successor leading-edge nodes on a go-forward basis. In such event, we may pause or discontinue our pursuit of Intel 14A and successor nodes and various of our manufacturing expansion projects."

Meanwhile, Taiwan Semiconductor expects its capital budget to be between $52B and $56B in 2026. The company noted that about 70% to 80% of the 2026 capital budget will be allocated to advanced process technologies. "In 2025, we spent USD 40.9 billion as compared to USD 29.8 billion in 2024 as we began to raise our level of capital spending in anticipation of the growth that will follow in the future years," said TSM's CFO Wendell Huang on the company's fourth quarter earnings call.

Huang noted that the company is facing increasing manufacturing cost challenges due to the rising cost of leading nodes.

"For example, the cost of tools are becoming more expensive and process complexity is increasing. As a result, the CapEx dollar required to build 1,000 wafer per month capacity of N2 is substantially higher than 1,000 wafer per month capacity for N3. The CapEx per k cost for A14 will be even higher," Huang added.

Huang noted that in the last three years, the company's CapEx dollars amount totaled $101B and is expected to be significantly higher in the next three years.

Spending on chip equipment tools is a positive for tool makers such as ASML (ASML), Applied Materials (AMAT), Lam Research (LRCX), and KLA (KLAC).

data.title: AT1 compromise for UBS opposed by Swiss finance minister
data.description:
Swiss Finance Minister Karin Keller-Sutter has come out against a proposed compromise in the debate over new capital rules for UBS Group (UBS), the country's largest lender.

In an interview with Swiss news outlet Finanz und Wirtschaft, Keller-Sutter said a plan floated by lawmakers to rely on AT1 bonds instead of full CET1 capital to back foreign subsidiaries fails to address the core risks. That prompted UBS (UBS) to explore selling two U.S. dollar-denominated AT1 bonds.

UBS (UBS) shares dipped 1.5% in afternoon trading.

Under the finance ministry's plan, UBS (UBS) would be required to fully capitalize its foreign subsidiaries through its Swiss unit, a move expected to increase the bank's capital needs by about $23B. UBS has opposed the government's proposal, which been criticized for being so strict that it would make Swiss banks non-competitive with other global banks. Lawmakers and regulators are weighing how much extra capital UBS should be required to hold after its emergency takeover of ex-rival Credit Suisse three years ago.

The AT1 plan “effectively wouldn’t be an improvement on today’s situation,” Keller-Sutter argued in the interview, noting the design of AT1s makes them unfit for the role being proposed.

The Swiss People's Party (SVP), Switzerland's largest political party, has backed the plan allowing UBS (UBS) to use AT1 to meet part of the new capital rules.

data.title: First Citizens signals $181B–$186B deposit growth target for 2026 while advancing tech investment and balance sheet optimization
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: First Citizens BancShares, Inc. (FCNCA) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Chairman & CEO Frank Holding reported adjusted earnings per share of $51.27, adjusted ROE of 11.93%, and adjusted ROA of 1.1%, emphasizing "these earnings metrics exceeded our expectations marked by resilient net interest income and stable credit quality."
  • Holding announced a change in operating segment reporting, with SVB Commercial consolidated into the Commercial Bank segment while continuing to report key SVB-specific metrics.
  • Loans were up $3.2 billion or 2.2% driven by global fund banking within SVB Commercial. Deposits declined $1.16 billion or 1%, attributed to shifts into off-balance sheet funds and seasonal effects, but average deposits rose $2.6 billion or 1.6%.
  • Holding highlighted return of $900 million to shareholders via share repurchases and progress toward long-term capital targets. Liquidity remains strong despite loan growth exceeding deposit growth and repayment of $2.5 billion of the FDIC Purchase Money Note.
  • Strategic priorities for 2026 remain: deepening client relationships, developing talent, optimizing the balance sheet, and investing in growth capabilities. There is a focus on enhancing payments capabilities to drive client growth and core deposit capture.
  • Holding announced the upcoming retirement of Chief Risk Officer Lorie Rupp in June 2026, with Treasurer Tom Eklund set to succeed her, citing his "substantial experience managing capital, market, liquidity and compliance risk."
  • CFO Craig Nix stated, "We had adjusted net income of $648 million in the fourth quarter or $51.27 per share, which was up $6.65 over the linked quarter driven mostly by a decline in provision for credit losses due to lower net charge-offs and a higher net provision release."
  • Nix added, "Tangible book value per share grew by 11% in 2025 and 3% sequentially, including the impacts from share repurchases."

Outlook

  • Nix projected first quarter 2026 loans in the $148 billion to $151 billion range, supported by optimism in Global Fund Banking, commercial finance, and middle market banking.
  • Full-year 2026 loan guidance is $153 billion to $157 billion.
  • Deposit guidance for Q1 2026 is $164 billion to $167 billion, with full-year target at $181 billion to $186 billion, including about $5.7 billion from the BMO branch acquisition in the second half of the year.
  • Headline net interest income for Q1 2026 is expected in the $1.6 billion to $1.7 billion range; full-year projection is $6.5 billion to $6.9 billion. Nix noted, "We project loan accretion will be down approximately $100 million for the year."
  • Adjusted noninterest income guidance is $500 million to $530 million for Q1, $2.1 billion to $2.2 billion for the full year.
  • Adjusted noninterest expense is expected at $1.34 billion to $1.38 billion for Q1 2026, and $5.37 billion to $5.46 billion for the full year, "a low to mid-single-digit percentage increase from 2025." The adjusted efficiency ratio is expected to be in the lower 60% range in 2026.
  • First quarter net charge-offs are anticipated to be 35 to 45 basis points, consistent with the prior range.

Financial Results

  • Adjusted net income was $648 million in Q4; adjusted EPS reached $51.27.
  • Tangible book value per share grew 11% year-over-year and 3% sequentially.
  • Headline net interest income declined $12 million sequentially, with NIM at 3.2%, down 6 basis points.
  • Adjusted noninterest income rose 2% sequentially, driven by rental and wealth management income, while client investment fees declined.
  • Adjusted noninterest expense increased $89 million, led by higher personnel, technology, and direct bank marketing costs.
  • Period-end loans increased by $3.2 billion, largely from Global Fund Banking, while deposits declined by $1.6 billion; average deposits increased $2.6 billion.
  • Provision for credit losses declined $137 million, with net charge-offs at $143 million.
  • Share repurchases totaled $900 million in Q4, with $4.9 billion completed since July 2024.
  • CET1 ratio at quarter-end was 11.15%, down 50 basis points from Q3.

Q&A

  • Christopher McGratty, KBW: Asked about rate cut assumptions and NII trajectory. CFO Nix responded, "our baseline forecast calls for 2 rate cuts in 2026 in June and October...we expect that headline and ex purchase accounting, net interest income will trough in the first quarter of '26."
  • McGratty also questioned whether technology and investment spend was peaking. CIO Gregory Smith replied, "we are indeed peaking in 2026 from a spend on the tech road map...at the end, we will not only have efficiency coming out of that example. But we'll also have a much better resiliency and more flexibility and scalability in our environment."
  • Anthony Elian, JPMorgan: Inquired about expense outlook range. Elliot Howard explained, "what puts us probably at the top end of that range is...heavy lift on direct bank advertising to really kind of raise deposits."
  • Elian also asked about SVB client fund growth. Marc Cadieux said growth was "a function of the incremental improvement we're seeing, venture investment, year-over-year innovation economy activity."
  • Casey Haire, Autonomous: Asked about purchase money note repayment pace. Treasurer Tom Eklund stated, "the $500 million to $1 billion would be sort of -- and you should think of that as sort of the minimum amount that we'd pay off this year."
  • Haire also queried on loan growth moderation. CEO Holding indicated liquidity needs and purchase money note repayment were factors, while Eklund explained that capital call line growth would lead to larger paydowns, moderating growth.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone focused on clarity for NII, expenses, and SVB growth, with questions pressing for specifics on loan growth, technology spend, and purchase money note strategy, reflecting a neutral to slightly cautious sentiment.
  • Management expressed confidence in outlook and strategic investments, while acknowledging cost pressures and macro headwinds. Nix used phrases like "we expect" and "we are optimistic," while also emphasizing cost discipline and efficiency.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone shifted from guarded optimism to more defined confidence in technology investments and deposit growth, while analysts maintained a probing but matter-of-fact approach.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q4 saw strong EPS and net income growth compared to Q3, with an explicit increase in tangible book value and continued share repurchases.
  • Loan growth in Q4 was robust but slightly lower in percentage terms than Q3, while deposit balances decreased at quarter-end due to seasonal and off-balance sheet shifts, though average deposits grew.
  • Management's guidance language for 2026 is more specific, with a focus on broad-based deposit growth and technology investment peaking.
  • The prior quarter featured more caution regarding the innovation economy and SVB outlook, whereas the current quarter signals growing confidence in SVB Commercial and Global Fund Banking.
  • Expense guidance has evolved from mid- to upper-single-digit growth in 2025 to low- to mid-single-digit for 2026, with a clearer message on cost curve "bending."
  • Analysts in both quarters pressed for clarity on NII and expenses, but management now offers more detailed forward guidance and strategic milestones.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited headwinds from lower rates, competitive pressure on lending spreads, and continued competition for deposits.
  • Elevated losses are projected to persist in the Commercial Bank general office portfolio and select equipment finance assets, with SVB Commercial losses to remain slightly elevated in early 2026.
  • Nix warned, "While losses in the equipment finance portfolio have largely stabilized, we have a larger deal we are watching that could elevate losses during the first quarter."
  • The company will continue to monitor macro and geopolitical uncertainties, with risk governance remaining a priority during executive transitions in the risk management function.

Final Takeaway

Management underscored that First Citizens enters 2026 with strong earnings momentum, a resilient balance sheet, and clear strategic priorities focused on deepening client relationships, investing in technology, and optimizing funding and capital. The company projects continued broad-based loan and deposit growth, with investments in digital capabilities and operational streamlining expected to drive long-term value, while maintaining rigorous risk management and cost discipline in a dynamic environment.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Packaging stocks jump as price increases hit market - report
data.description:
Packaging stocks rose Friday following a report that Packaging Corp. of America (PKG) plans to raise prices by $70/ton, effective March 1, citing letters sent to containerboard buyers.

International Paper (IP) +3.4%, Packaging Corp. of America (PKG) +2.8%, and Smurfit WestRock (SW) +2.8%, all spiking due to the report.

The move marks another round in the industry’s efforts to lift margins amid a sluggish demand environment and tight labor conditions, according to the report.
